epigraph.quote: The rooms you were not in are the reason the room you were in existed.

blocks.text: Before you sat in the casting chair — before the camera rolled — before the script was in your hands —
what do you think was happening?
She answers. Development. Pre-production. He walks her through what those words actually mean.
The production process is not a game. It is not an argument. It is not a debate.
And it is the producer who pays all of it.
The actor who uses their platform to pressure a producer — who renegotiates after an agreement from a position of leverage —
calls this creative control. Artistic integrity. Knowing their worth.
I have a different name for it.
a beat — the word arriving without drama
The producer believed in you enough to build their project around you. And some actors respond to that belief by using it against the person who held it.
The triangle on the desk between them.
The producer sees the whole. The actor inhabits the part.
These are not competing authorities. They are complementary ones.
The question that resolves all disagreement:
The Apprentice looks at the triangle. Then:
The producer paid for the room. The author wrote what the room was for. My job is to make the room worth what it cost.
a beat — arriving at the full thing
Not for the producer. For the audience who will sit in it.
The producer who made your first production possible — do you know what they did before you arrived? The rooms you were not in are the reason the room you were in existed.
Know what the producer did. Before you arrived. Know what it cost. And bring to the partnership what only you can bring — without using it as a weapon.

requirement.prompt: Post about a production you have been part of. Name one thing the producer did that you did not know about at the time — that you understand now. What did it cost them? Include #JoelhoodP4L3.
